main_summary.summary: Reverend Dr. Amos C. Brown, interviewed by David Cline in San Francisco on March 2, 2013, recounts his civil rights work from Jackson, Mississippi through the NAACP Youth Council network, Morehouse College, and Crozer Theological Seminary into his pastorate at Third Baptist Church San Francisco. Born February 20, 1941, Brown traces his lineage through Patrick Brown, a slave in Franklin County, Mississippi near the Forks of the Road slave market east of Natchez. He describes the 1955 murders of Reverend George Lee and Emmett Till, the Eddie Noel shooting in Holmes County, and Arenia Mallory's role at the Saints Industrial School in Lexington. Mentored by Medgar Evers beginning in 1955, Brown organized the first Mississippi NAACP Youth Council, traveled with Evers to the 1956 NAACP National Convention in San Francisco, was elected National Chairman of the NAACP Youth Work Committee in 1959, and was punished by Jackson school authorities including loss of his valedictorian honor at Jim Hill High School. He studied with Martin Luther King Jr. at Morehouse, joined the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights in Atlanta, and was arrested at the Livingston Park Zoo during the 1961 Freedom Rides. He has pastored Third Baptist Church San Francisco since 1976.
key_themes: NAACP Youth Council organizing in Mississippi
Civil rights and gay rights coalition advocacy
main_summary.historical_significance:
Brown's testimony documents the NAACP Youth Council pipeline that connected Mississippi, Oklahoma City, and Greensboro sit-in movements, including his claim that the student sit-down movement began with Clara Luper's Oklahoma City youth on August 28, 1958 rather than in Greensboro on February 1, 1960. He provides first-hand corroboration of the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission's surveillance of teenage civil rights organizers, naming bank vice president LATAM as a source of confidential financial information passed to Mayor Thompson and Jackson police.

As the last surviving member of the Atlanta Committee on Appeal for Human Rights that included Lonnie King, Charles Black, Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Marian Wright Edelman, Brown bridges the Mississippi NAACP youth network, the Morehouse-King mentorship, and the Crozer Theological Seminary pipeline shaped by J. Pius Barber. His subsequent advocacy linking civil rights to LGBTQ rights, refugee resettlement of Ethiopian and Eritrean refugees through Third Baptist, and Operation Crossroads Africa work extends the movement framework into the post-1965 era.

chapters.title: Mississippi Roots and Slave Lineage
chapters.summary: Brown describes being born in Jackson, Mississippi on February 20, 1941, and traces his great-great-grandfather Patrick Brown to slavery in Franklin County's Hamburg community, near the Forks of the Road slave market east of Natchez. He references the cholera epidemic of 1830 that quarantined incoming slaves at that site and notes his father's pastoral work in the Mississippi Delta succeeding the murdered Reverend George Lee.

chapters.title: Eddie Noel and Childhood Awakening
chapters.summary: Brown recounts the 1955 case of Eddie Noel, a World War II veteran sharpshooter from Holmes County who killed three white men and evaded a 300-400 person posse. Arenia Mallory, first principal of the Saints Industrial School and vice president of the National Council of Negro Women, persuaded Noel to surrender. Brown notes Noel was committed to Whitfield Mental Institution rather than tried and was a descendant of Governor Edmund Noel.

chapters.title: Medgar Evers and Youth Council Organizing
chapters.summary: Brown describes Miss Essie Collins, treasurer of the Jackson NAACP branch, naming him after the prophet Amos at age eight at Farish Street Baptist Church. He learned of Channing H. Tobias's 1954 "Free by '63" declaration through his sister Gloria's report after she attended the Dallas NAACP meeting with Medgar Evers. Brown organized the first NAACP Youth Council in Mississippi in 1955 and traveled with Evers to the 1956 San Francisco National Convention in the Blue Goose Oldsmobile.

chapters.title: Third Baptist and Movement Churches
chapters.summary: Brown recounts worshipping at Third Baptist Church on his 1956 San Francisco trip and later being called as its pastor in 1976 to succeed Dr. Leon Howard Sullivan. He places the church's 1852 founding by Eliza Davis and her husband William alongside Harriet Beecher Stowe's Uncle Tom's Cabin and the abolition of Caribbean slavery. He names the four Jackson churches that opened their doors to civil rights activity: Farish Street Baptist, Pearl Street AME, College Hill Baptist, and New Mount Zion Baptist.

chapters.title: Sovereignty Commission Surveillance
chapters.summary: Brown details the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission's 10-page file on him, opened in the 1980s, including reports that a deposit guaranteed bank vice president named LATAM leaked his account information to Mayor Thompson and Jackson police. He describes the Commission's practice of recording license plates at mass meetings and pressuring employers to fire civil rights workers, naming Mr. Kirby P. Walker as the white superintendent who attempted to bar him from re-enrolling at Jim Hill High School after his 1958 Cleveland Plain Dealer interview.

chapters.title: Oklahoma City and the Sit-In Origin Claim
chapters.summary: Brown identifies the August 28, 1958 Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Council sit-ins led by Clara Luper as the true origin of the student sit-down movement, predating Greensboro by 17 months. He recounts presiding at the 1959 NAACP New York meeting where Ezell Blair Jr. heard Luper's report and carried the model to A&T College, sparking the February 1, 1960 Greensboro action. Brown notes the credentials committee record at the Library of Congress as documentation.

chapters.title: Morehouse and the Atlanta Movement
chapters.summary: Brown describes his Morehouse years, including studying with Martin Luther King Jr., desegregating First Baptist Church Atlanta where he met his wife at Spelman, and authoring with Lonnie King, Charles Black, Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Marian Wright Edelman the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights document published in the Atlanta Journal Constitution. He recalls Drs. Rufus Clement and Benjamin Mays funding the publication and his subsequent jailing with King, A.D. King, Otis Moss, and David Satcher.
keywords: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights

chapters.title: International Exposure and Crozer Pipeline
chapters.summary: Brown describes meeting Rajmohan Gandhi in 1957 through the Moral Rearmament movement and attending Frank Buchman's gatherings at Mackinac Island. He details his 1958 Quaker world affairs camp at Cornell University and his 1964 Operation Crossroads Africa trip to Liberia, Sierra Leone, Senegal, and Ghana. He places Crozer Theological Seminary in the lineage of Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, J. Pius Barber, Samuel D. Proctor, Harold Carter, William Augustus Jones, Charles Booth, Jesse Wendell Mapson, and Archie LeMone.

chapters.title: Personalism and LGBTQ Rights Advocacy
chapters.summary: Brown recounts King's Morehouse seminar on personalism, the Brightman-derived doctrine that every human being possesses dignity and worth. He applies this to his ordination of women preachers since 1984 and his opposition to Proposition 8 on California's marriage equality ballot, citing John Mercer Langston's 1866 St. Louis speech and Charles Satchel, the founding 1852 pastor of Third Baptist. He recalls a young congregant attempting to take his pulpit when he refused to support Proposition 8.

chapters.title: Human Rights and Africa Work
chapters.summary: Brown extends his framework from civil rights to global human rights, citing King's Birmingham jail letter and Leakey's Rift Valley paleontology. He recounts Third Baptist's resettlement of approximately 3,000 Ethiopian, Eritrean, and Caribbean refugees beginning in his own family room during the 1970s. He co-founded the African-American Summit with Dr. Leon Sullivan in 1990, first held in Abidjan, Ivory Coast in 1991. He describes mentoring Ghanaian musician Kofi who he met at Ghana's 50th independence anniversary.

chapters.title: 1961 Freedom Rides and Evers Assassination
chapters.summary: Brown recounts his 1961 arrest at the Livingston Park Zoo in Jackson for protesting segregated benches and his subsequent arrest after taking an 85-year-old neighbor to the University of Mississippi Hospital where the doctor called him "boy." He describes learning of Medgar Evers's June 1963 assassination while in summer school at Morehouse, attending the Jackson funeral, and witnessing police on horseback beating mourners with billy clubs as the procession marched before Evers's burial at Arlington National Cemetery.

chapters.title: Gun Violence and Mississippi's Unsung Heroes
chapters.summary: Brown discusses his recent meeting at the Biden vice presidential office with Franklin Graham and Richard Land on gun violence following the Newtown shooting, citing Anna Green from the 2011 Tucson shooting. He concludes by naming Mississippi's unsung organizers: C.C. Bryant of McComb, Amzie Moore of Clarksdale, Samuel Bailey, Joseph Broadwater, Carsie Hall, Reverend William Albert Bender of Tougaloo who fought for equal teacher pay in 1946, and his daughter Lou Ella Bender. He references Andrew Delbanco's The Real American Dream and the 2012 Django Unchained film.

loc_video.caption: Amos C. Brown oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in San Francisco, California, 2013 March 02
